moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with the Van Geet Opening, an unusual but creative choice that immediately develops a piece and controls the central d5 and e4 squares. By playing the knight before pawns, we keep flexibility and avoid the main-line openings where Black has deep preparation. This move puts immediate pressure on d5 and prepares to support a pawn advance—notice how the knight eyes e4, which will become important soon.

Black strikes in the center immediately, challenging our setup and claiming space. This is a natural response that forces us to make a decision: will we defend e4 or advance it? Black correctly recognizes that we can't maintain a passive position and invites us to reveal our tactical ideas.

We push through with our gambit idea—sacrificing the e4 pawn to open lines and activate our pieces aggressively. The pawn on c3 supports this advance, and we're counting on rapid development and the open lines to compensate for the pawn. This is the Dougherty Gambit, and it's named specifically because of this aggressive pawn push that gives Black a choice: take the pawn or allow us to develop with tempo.

Black captures the pawn, which is the critical line and the position where the gambit is truly tested. Taking on e4 is natural and greedy—after all, we've offered a pawn. Now we need to show that our next move justifies the sacrifice by creating serious threats and counterplay against Black's newly centralized pawn.

We attack the pawn on e4 directly and prepare to recapture it while opening the f-file for our rook and creating threats along the long diagonal. The move f3 also supports a potential d4 push, which would reassert central control and complete our development plan. This is where the gambit's compensation becomes clear—our pieces are poised for an aggressive setup while Black's e4 pawn becomes a target to defend.
